pages.raw_text:
NORTH YAKIMA. Jan. 9.-The
coal famine in this city was broken
temporarily yesterday by a whole
sale confiscation of coal from the
Northern Pacific trains. Hundreds
Of citizens participated in the seiz-
ure and no effort to interfere was
made by the authorities. Notifica-
ton of such action had been pre-
viously been served on the COM
pany.


Citizens of all classes, including
many business men, climbed onto
the cars and opened the dumps,
letting the coal fal onto the tracks,
thus preventing. the trains from
moving out. Wagons by the score
were on hand and were hastily
loaded, while those without con-
veyances filled sacks and bore
them home to replenish empty
stoves.


Probably Is carloads were con-
fiscated and in the confusion much
of it was carried off without any
record being made so that the
company will be unable to collect
payment in these instances.


Citizens waiting for coal were ex-
asperated when it was learned that
but two cars out of train that
pulled in were consigned to this
place. They had permitted one
train to go through, but refused to
take chances on losing any more.
Everybody was able to keep warm
in this city last night for the first
time in several days.
